back: To verify the countable/co-countable construction, split disjoint families into the cases where all sets are countable or exactly one set is co-countable. Use case: Exercise 1.1.1 and any probability-space verification on unusual sigma-fields.

Show half-open rectangles are Borel, then express rational open rectangles as countable unions of half-open rectangles; use the countable rational base. Use case: Exercises 1.1.2 and 1.1.3; product-measure generation arguments.

front: Increasing union of sigma-fields can fail countable closure
back: Use finite initial-coordinate sigma-fields on N; their union contains every finite set but misses an infinite non-cofinite set such as the evens. Use case: Exercise 1.1.4; distinguishing algebras from sigma-fields.

front: Block construction for asymptotic density counterexamples
back: Use rapidly growing blocks so the latest block dominates all previous blocks; alternate behavior by blocks to destroy the density of intersections. Use case: Exercise 1.1.5 and density/limit counterexamples.
For Z = X on A and Y on A^c, write {Z <= x} as (A cap {X <= x}) union (A^c cap {Y <= x}). Use case: Exercise 1.2.1; patching random variables on measurable events.
Group jumps by size bigger than 1/m inside bounded intervals; each group is finite because total CDF increase is at most one. Use case: Exercise 1.2.3; monotone-function discontinuity arguments.
For Y=g(X) with g increasing, compute F_Y(y)=P(X <= g^{-1}(y)) and differentiate to get f_X(g^{-1}(y))/g'(g^{-1}(y)). Use case: Exercises 1.2.5 and 1.2.6; lognormal and affine density transforms.
For Y=X^2, use F_Y(y)=P(-sqrt(y)<=X<=sqrt(y)) and differentiate to get [f(sqrt(y))+f(-sqrt(y))]/(2sqrt(y)). Use case: Exercise 1.2.7; chi-square with one degree of freedom.

front: Rational cut proof for sum measurability
back: Write {X+Y<x} as the countable union over q in Q of {X<q} cap {Y<x-q}. Use case: Exercise 1.3.2; direct measurability of sums.

front: Lower semicontinuity via closed sublevel sets
back: A function is lower semicontinuous iff all sets {f <= a} are closed; sequential closure proves one direction and contradiction via subsequences proves the other. Use case: Exercise 1.3.5; proving semicontinuous functions are measurable.

front: Discontinuity set from upper and lower local envelopes
back: Define local sup and inf over balls, take limits as radius decreases, and identify discontinuities as the set where the limiting envelopes differ. Use case: Exercise 1.3.6; measurability of discontinuity sets.

Quantize Y into dyadic bins, represent each bin as {X in B}, build Borel simple functions f_n, then take a measurable pointwise limit f with Y=f(X). Use case: Exercises 1.3.8 and 1.3.9; conditional-expectation foundations.

front: Zero integral of nonnegative function implies zero a.e.
back: Use sets {f > 1/n}; each has zero measure because integral dominates (1/n) times its measure, and their union is {f>0}. Use case: Exercise 1.4.1; null-set proofs.
Approximate f>=0 by 2^{-n} floor(2^n f); the approximants increase to f and their integrals give monotone sums over dyadic level sets. Use case: Exercise 1.4.2 and simple-function approximation.

Approximate an integrable function by simple functions, approximate measurable sets by finite interval unions, then round step-function corners. Use case: Exercise 1.4.3; density of nice functions in L1 on R.
Prove oscillatory integral decay for interval indicators explicitly, then approximate any L1 function by a step function. Use case: Exercise 1.4.4; oscillatory integral estimates.
If |g| <= ||g||_infty a.e., then |fg| <= ||g||_infty |f| and integration gives the L1-Linfty Holder endpoint. Use case: Exercise 1.5.1; endpoint Holder inequalities.

front: Lp norms converge to Linfty on probability spaces
back: Upper bound by essential supremum; lower bound using sets where |f| exceeds M-epsilon and their positive probability. Use case: Exercise 1.5.2; norm comparisons.

front: Simple functions are dense in Lp
back: Use pointwise simple approximations bounded by |f|, then dominated convergence on |phi_n-f|^p. Use case: Exercise 1.5.9; Lp approximation.

front: Exchange integral and absolutely summable series
back: If sum integral |f_n| is finite, then H=sum |f_n| is integrable; dominated convergence applies to partial sums. Use case: Exercise 1.5.10 and 1.7.1 corollary.
For strictly convex phi, equality in Jensen forces the random variable to be almost surely constant at its mean. Use case: Exercise 1.6.1; strict convexity equality cases.
To prove sharpness of moment/tail inequalities, place small mass at boundary points and the rest at a value chosen to match moments. Use case: Exercises 1.6.3, 1.6.4, and 1.6.5.

front: Cauchy-Schwarz lower bound for positive probability
back: For Y>=0, Cauchy-Schwarz applied to Y*1_{Y>0} yields P(Y>0) >= (EY)^2/EY^2. Use case: Exercise 1.6.6; basic Paley-Zygmund-style estimates.
Control small or large tails by splitting the event and using continuity of probability plus elementary bounds on the integrand. Use case: Exercise 1.6.14; limits involving y E(1/X; X>y).

front: Indicator expansion for inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni
back: Use 1_union = 1 - product_i(1-1_Ai), expand, and take expectations; partial alternating sums give Bonferroni bounds. Use case: Exercises 1.6.9 and 1.6.10.
Represent g(x) as the vertical length of {(x,y):0 <= y < g(x)} and apply Tonelli to integrate sections. Use case: Exercise 1.7.2; tail integrals for nonnegative functions.

front: Stieltjes integration by parts with jump correction
back: Interpret Stieltjes integrals as product-measure masses of order regions; the diagonal overlap contributes the product of jump masses. Use case: Exercise 1.7.3.
Write F(x+c)-F(x) as mu((x,x+c]), swap integrals, and observe each fixed t is counted for exactly c units of x. Use case: Exercise 1.7.4.
Represent sin(x)/x as an integral of e^{-xy} sin(x), integrate in x first, and bound the remaining exponentially damped tails. Use case: Exercise 1.7.5.
